concerns.text: FR-014 mandates a power analysis for PERMANOVA with a flag if n < 10 per group. However, US-001 only guarantees a total of ≥10 samples across ALL groups (early, intermediate, mature). In a 3-group design, this yields n≈3 per group. PERMANOVA relies on within-group dispersion estimates; n=3 is statistically insufficient to distinguish community differences from noise, rendering the 'underpowered' flag a near-certainty that halts the primary analysis. The design guarantees a sample size that cannot support the intended statistical test.

US-003 and FR-006 propose constructing co-occurrence networks using Spearman correlations with a threshold of |ρ|≥0.6 on a dataset where n (samples) is likely < 15 (based on US-001's ≥10 total). In microbial ecology, correlation-based network inference is notoriously unstable and prone to false positives when n << p (number of taxa). With n < 15, the correlation matrix is rank-deficient and the resulting network topology (modularity, hubs) is mathematically determined by sampling noise rather than biological signal. The spec treats the network as a valid output without acknowledging that the sample size makes the inference methodologically invalid for this specific regime.

FR-012 requires k-fold cross-validation (k=5) on the taxa-nutrient correlation model. With a total sample size of ~10-15 (US-001), a 5-fold CV results in training sets of only 2-3 samples per fold. This is insufficient to train a stable regression model or to meaningfully validate predictive power, as the model will be overfit to the tiny training set and the validation set will be too small to estimate error variance. The method is unsound for the data volume guaranteed by the spec.
